title: Monitoring Tools Pricing Comparison for 2026
description: How much does infrastructure and application monitoring cost in 2026? Compare pricing models across Datadog, New Relic, Grafana, Sentry, and more — covering per-host, per-event, and per-user pricing structures.
body:
Monitoring tools are among the most expensive categories in the developer toolchain for teams operating at scale. The pricing models vary dramatically — per-host, per-event, per-user, and per-data-ingested — and the cost of monitoring a 50-server fleet ranges from free to tens of thousands of dollars per month depending on the tool and tier selected.

This comparison examines the pricing models for the major monitoring and observability platforms. All pricing is based on published rates at the time of writing. Check each vendor's current pricing page for exact figures.

## Datadog Pricing

Datadog uses a per-host pricing model for its infrastructure monitoring, with separate charges for each additional product — APM, logs, database monitoring, network performance, and synthetic monitoring. The Free tier provides basic monitoring with limited data retention and fewer integrations. Paid tiers add longer data retention, advanced alerting, and team collaboration features. Enterprise pricing includes custom contract terms, dedicated support, and advanced security controls.

Datadog's pricing advantage is the breadth of the platform — a single agent collects infrastructure, application, and network data, reducing the operational overhead of maintaining multiple monitoring tools. The cost tradeoff is that each additional Datadog product adds separate charges, and teams that enable multiple products face significant cost accumulation.

Datadog's pricing trap is the per-host cost escalation as the fleet grows. Each host added to the monitoring scope increases the bill linearly, and teams with large ephemeral environments — container-based deployments with frequent host churn — can face unexpected cost spikes.

Check Datadog's current pricing page for exact per-host rates on Pro and Enterprise tiers.

## New Relic Pricing

New Relic transitioned from a per-server pricing model to a per-user model with data ingestion limits. The Free tier provides 100 GB of data ingestion per month with one full-platform user and basic APM features. Paid tiers increase data ingestion limits, add team members, and unlock advanced capabilities such as AI-driven alerts, code-level visibility, and custom dashboards. Enterprise pricing is custom with dedicated support and advanced compliance features.

New Relic's pricing advantage is the per-user model for teams with small monitoring teams but large infrastructure. A 500-host environment monitored by a 5-person SRE team pays based on 5 users rather than 500 hosts, which is cost-effective compared to per-host models.

New Relic's pricing trap is the data ingestion limit. Teams that exceed the included data volume face overage charges that can double the base subscription cost. Monitoring teams must carefully estimate log and metric volume before selecting a tier.

Check New Relic's current pricing page for exact rates and data ingestion limits on paid tiers.

## Grafana Pricing

Grafana provides open-source self-hosted monitoring with optional Grafana Cloud paid tiers. The open-source version is free but requires infrastructure management. Grafana Cloud offers free, paid, and enterprise tiers with increasing data retention, user limits, and support levels.

Grafana's pricing advantage is the open-source base — teams with the engineering capacity to self-host can use Grafana for free with unlimited data retention and users. The cloud tiers provide a managed alternative for teams that prefer to avoid operational overhead.

Grafana's pricing trap is the self-hosted operational cost. Teams that choose the free self-hosted option must budget for server infrastructure, database management, backup configurations, and upgrade maintenance — costs that are invisible in the product pricing but real in the total cost of ownership.

Check Grafana Labs' current pricing page for exact rates on Cloud Pro, Advanced, and Enterprise tiers.

## Sentry Pricing

Sentry provides error tracking and performance monitoring with a per-event pricing model. The Free tier offers a generous allocation of error events and performance transactions per month. Paid tiers increase event quotas, add team members, and unlock advanced features such as session replay, distributed tracing, and code-level insights. Enterprise pricing includes custom event quotas, dedicated support, and advanced security.

Sentry's pricing advantage is the developer-focused workflow — error grouping, issue assignment, and release tracking are built into the developer workflow rather than bolted on as a separate monitoring concern.

Sentry's pricing trap is the per-event overage. Teams with high-traffic applications can exhaust their event quota quickly, and the cost of additional event blocks adds up faster than many teams expect.

Check Sentry's current pricing page for exact rates on Team, Business, and Enterprise tiers.

## Pricing Model Comparison

The monitoring tool landscape uses three primary pricing models. Per-host pricing (Datadog) scales with infrastructure size and is most cost-effective for teams with a small number of powerful hosts. Per-user pricing (New Relic) scales with team size and is most cost-effective for teams with many hosts but few monitoring engineers. Per-event pricing (Sentry) scales with application traffic and is most cost-effective for teams with moderate traffic but complex error monitoring needs. The right model depends on which dimension — hosts, users, or events — is the most constrained in the team's environment.

## Cost Control Strategies

Regardless of which monitoring tool a team selects, cost control requires active management. Teams should set alerting thresholds on usage to detect unexpected ingestion spikes, configure sampling for high-volume logs and traces, and regularly audit which hosts, services, and team members are consuming monitoring resources. The teams that manage monitoring costs most effectively treat monitoring spend as a variable cost that requires monthly review rather than a fixed subscription.
author: PilotStack Team
